papers.title: Enabling superior impact toughness of low-density δ-ferrite steel by dispersing ultra-fine spheroidized carbides in the ferrite/carbide composite lamellae
papers.paper_scope: Investigate the synergistic effect of rolling temperature on microstructural evolution, work hardening behavior, and impact toughness in low-density δ-ferrite steel, establishing the correlation between ultra-fine spherical carbides in ferrite/carbide composite lamellae and crack propagation mechanisms.

alloys.alloys_notes: Fabricated via vacuum induction melting (VIM). High Al content (~4.5 wt%) expands the δ-ferrite phase region, stabilizing coarse δ-ferrite at room temperature.

processes.description: Homogenized at 1200°C for 4 h, hot forged to 40 mm and air cooled; reheated to 650°C for 2 h, hot rolled with 60% reduction from 40 mm to 16 mm, air cooled.
processes.processes_notes: Low-temperature rolling within carbide precipitation stability range (<750°C), enabling ultra-fine spherical carbide formation.

processes.description: Homogenized at 1200°C for 4 h, hot forged to 40 mm and air cooled; reheated to 750°C for 2 h, hot rolled with 60% reduction from 40 mm to 16 mm, air cooled.
processes.processes_notes: Low-temperature rolling near upper limit of carbide stability, resulting in coarsened carbides via Ostwald ripening.

processes.description: Homogenized at 1200°C for 4 h, hot forged to 40 mm and air cooled; reheated to 850°C for 2 h, hot rolled with 60% reduction from 40 mm to 16 mm, air cooled.
processes.processes_notes: High-temperature rolling within DRX and DIFT range (≥850°C); carbides fully dissolve, forming δ-ferrite and honeycomb martensite lamellae with DIF.

processes.description: Homogenized at 1200°C for 4 h, hot forged to 40 mm and air cooled; reheated to 950°C for 2 h, hot rolled with 60% reduction from 40 mm to 16 mm, air cooled.
processes.processes_notes: High-temperature rolling with increased austenite stability; higher martensite fraction (59.0%) and coarsened DIF grains.

structures.overall_structure: Lamellar structure with alternating δ-ferrite lamellae (light areas) and carbide/ferrite (C/F) composite lamellae (dark areas), both showing continuous banded distribution parallel to the rolling direction.

phases_present.morphology: lamellar, continuous bands parallel to RD
lamellar bands containing ultra-fine spherical carbides dispersed in ferrite matrix

grain_structure.texture: HAGBs proportion 49.6 %, GOS≤2° 12.3 %, average KAM ~0.48–0.55°, dominant orientation <001>

precipitates.distribution: 1.7 ± 0.2 μm^-2; ultra-fine spherical, high-density dispersed within ferrite matrix of composite lamellae; mainly concentrated outside δ-ferrite lamellae

structures.overall_structure: Lamellar structure with alternating δ-ferrite lamellae and carbide/ferrite composite lamellae; carbides exhibit coarsened distribution of coarse particles at grain boundaries and fine particles inside grains.

phases_present.morphology: lamellar, continuous bands parallel to RD
lamellar bands with coarsened spherical carbides in ferrite matrix

grain_structure.texture: HAGBs proportion 43.5 %, GOS≤2° 6.9 %, average KAM ~0.48–0.55°, dominant orientation <001>

precipitates.distribution: 0.6 ± 0.1 μm^-2; distribution characteristic of coarse carbides at grain boundaries plus fine carbides inside grains due to Ostwald ripening

structures.overall_structure: Dual-phase lamellar structure consisting of δ-ferrite lamellae and honeycomb martensite lamellae containing equiaxed deformation-induced ferrite (DIF) grains; carbides completely dissolved.

phases_present.morphology: equiaxed grains enclosed within honeycomb martensite lamellae

grain_structure.texture: HAGBs proportion 60.1 %, GOS≤2° 19.4 %, average KAM ~0.3–0.4°, dominant orientation shifting to <101>

structures.overall_structure: Dual-phase lamellar structure consisting of δ-ferrite lamellae and honeycomb martensite lamellae containing equiaxed DIF grains that grew significantly; carbides completely dissolved.

phases_present.morphology: equiaxed grains enclosed within honeycomb martensite lamellae, grown significantly compared to R850

grain_structure.texture: HAGBs proportion 75.7 %, GOS≤2° 25.8 %, average KAM ~0.3–0.4°, dominant orientation <101>

defect_interaction.interaction_type: dislocation pinning by spherical carbides (Orowan mechanism)
defect_interaction.quality_description: Carbides pin dislocations and increase work hardening rate of composite lamellae; part of interfacial stress between ferrite matrix and carbides is transferred to carbides, alleviating stress concentration.

interface_notes.stress_strain_distribution: During impact, interface separation occurs at lamellar boundaries dispersing crack-tip stress on the separation surface; spherical carbides increase effective curvature radius of crack tip reducing stress concentration factor according to elasticity theory.

defect_interaction.interaction_type: dislocation pinning by spherical carbides (Orowan mechanism)
defect_interaction.quality_description: Weakened Orowan effect due to carbide coarsening and reduced distribution density; coarse particles have weakened ability to disperse stress at crack tip.

interface_notes.stress_strain_distribution: Coarse carbides at grain boundaries and fine carbides inside grains due to Ostwald ripening; reduced crack deflection resistance and lower number of microvoid nucleation sites compared to R650.

defect_interaction.interaction_type: stress concentration at phase boundaries due to hardness mismatch
defect_interaction.quality_description: Maximum hardness difference reaches 3.86 GPa (martensite 6.0 GPa vs δ-ferrite 2.6 GPa), causing stress concentration at phase boundaries and preferential crack initiation sites.

interface_notes.stress_strain_distribution: Large hardness mismatch between martensite, DIF, and δ-ferrite induces stress concentration at phase interfaces; equiaxed DIF grains act as pre-existing defects guiding rapid cleavage crack propagation.

interfaces.phase_evolution: Carbides completely dissolved at high temperature; austenite stabilized by Mn and high temperature; after rolling, residual austenite transformed to martensite, resulting in honeycomb martensite lamellae with embedded equiaxed DIF grains.

defect_interaction.interaction_type: stress concentration at phase boundaries due to hardness mismatch
defect_interaction.quality_description: Maximum hardness difference reaches 3.86 GPa (martensite 6.5 GPa vs δ-ferrite 2.8 GPa), causing stress concentration at phase boundaries and brittle fracture behavior.

interface_notes.stress_strain_distribution: Martensite/ferrite hardness mismatch >3.3 GPa hinders deformation coordination; stress concentration at phase boundaries combined with DIF grains acting as pre-existing defects leads to unstable crack propagation immediately after initiation.

interfaces.phase_evolution: Increased martensite fraction (59.0%) and DIF grain coarsening compared to R850; high-temperature rolling significantly degrades toughness through microstructure coarsening and brittle phase formation.

characterization_methods.technique: SEM and EBSD (Oxford Aztec detector on Apreo S HiVac SEM)
characterization_methods.purpose: Microstructure, grain boundary, GOS, KAM, and IPF mapping on RD-ND plane
characterization_methods.key_findings: HAGBs proportion 49.6 %, GOS≤2° 12.3 %, average KAM ~0.48–0.55° corresponding to dislocation density 3.9×10^14 m^-2, dominant orientation <001>.

characterization_methods.technique: TEM and HRTEM (JEM-F200 CF-HR) with SAED and EDS
characterization_methods.purpose: High-resolution observation of carbides and dislocation structures
characterization_methods.key_findings: Ultra-fine spherical carbides (158.3±76.9 nm) dispersed in ferrite matrix; confirmed M3C, M23C6, and M7C3 carbides concentrated outside δ-ferrite lamellae; dislocations blocked by carbides (Orowan mechanism).

characterization_methods.purpose: Phase-specific hardness measurement aligned with EBSD statistics
characterization_methods.key_findings: C/F composite lamella hardness 3.6±0.6 GPa; δ-ferrite lamella hardness 2.9±0.3 GPa; hardness difference 0.72 GPa.

characterization_methods.technique: SEM and EBSD (Oxford Aztec detector on Apreo S HiVac SEM)
characterization_methods.purpose: Microstructure, grain boundary, GOS, KAM, and IPF mapping on RD-ND plane
characterization_methods.key_findings: HAGBs proportion 43.5 %, GOS≤2° 6.9 %, average KAM ~0.48–0.55° corresponding to dislocation density 3.4×10^14 m^-2, dominant orientation <001>.

characterization_methods.technique: TEM and HRTEM (JEM-F200 CF-HR) with SAED and EDS
characterization_methods.purpose: High-resolution observation of carbides and dislocation structures
characterization_methods.key_findings: Coarsened spherical carbides (247.3±86.8 nm) with distribution density 0.6±0.1 μm^-2; characteristic coarse carbides at grain boundaries plus fine carbides inside grains (Ostwald ripening).

characterization_methods.purpose: Phase-specific hardness measurement aligned with EBSD statistics
characterization_methods.key_findings: C/F composite lamella hardness 3.6±0.5 GPa; δ-ferrite lamella hardness 2.9±0.2 GPa.

characterization_methods.technique: SEM and EBSD (Oxford Aztec detector on Apreo S HiVac SEM)
characterization_methods.purpose: Microstructure, grain boundary, GOS, KAM, and IPF mapping on RD-ND plane
characterization_methods.key_findings: HAGBs proportion 60.1 %, GOS≤2° 19.4 %, average KAM ~0.3–0.4° corresponding to dislocation density 2.1×10^14 m^-2, orientation shifting toward <101>.

characterization_methods.purpose: High-resolution observation of martensite and DIF microstructure
characterization_methods.key_findings: Spherical carbides completely disappeared; honeycomb martensite lamellae with equiaxed DIF grains inside; no carbides observed.

characterization_methods.purpose: Phase-specific hardness measurement aligned with EBSD statistics
characterization_methods.key_findings: Martensite hardness 6.0±0.5 GPa; δ-ferrite hardness 2.6±0.3 GPa; DIF hardness 3.0±0.3 GPa; maximum hardness difference 3.86 GPa.

characterization_methods.technique: SEM and EBSD (Oxford Aztec detector on Apreo S HiVac SEM)
characterization_methods.purpose: Microstructure, grain boundary, GOS, KAM, and IPF mapping on RD-ND plane
characterization_methods.key_findings: HAGBs proportion 75.7 %, GOS≤2° 25.8 %, average KAM ~0.3–0.4° corresponding to dislocation density 2.8×10^14 m^-2, dominant orientation <101>.

characterization_methods.purpose: High-resolution observation of martensite and DIF microstructure
characterization_methods.key_findings: No carbides; honeycomb martensite lamellae with significantly grown equiaxed DIF grains; δ-ferrite fraction 41.0% and martensite fraction 59.0%.

characterization_methods.purpose: Phase-specific hardness measurement aligned with EBSD statistics
characterization_methods.key_findings: Martensite hardness 6.5±0.5 GPa; δ-ferrite hardness 2.8±0.2 GPa; DIF hardness 2.8±0.4 GPa; maximum hardness difference 3.86 GPa.

computational_details.validation_against_experiment: Predicted carbide stability below 750°C and δ+γ two-phase region at high temperatures, consistent with observed microstructures (complete carbide dissolution at ≥850°C, presence of austenite at 850–950°C).

analytical_model.equation_role: Explain dislocation pinning and strengthening by ultra-fine spherical carbides in ferrite matrix
analytical_model.model_note: Carbides with diameter <250 nm pin dislocations, increasing work hardening rate and crack propagation resistance in composite lamellae; effective for crack tip blunting when diameter <250 nm.

analytical_model.equation_role: Explain interface bonding and crack propagation resistance at carbide/ferrite interfaces
analytical_model.model_note: Bonding force at carbide/ferrite interface exceeds ferrite/ferrite interface; nanoscale spherical carbides increase crack tip curvature radius reducing stress concentration factor inversely proportional to radius.

unmapped_findings: As-forged control microstructure (before rolling) showed no obvious lamellar structure, consisting mainly of coarse δ-ferrite and M/A constituents (Fig. 3a-c).

R650 Charpy impact test detailed energy partition: crack initiation energy Ei = 59.6 J, propagation energy Ep = 147.9 J; R750 Ep = 51.51 J. High-temperature rolled specimens (R850, R950) exhibited steep single-peak impact load-displacement curves with immediate unstable crack propagation after initiation.
Strength-plasticity product (SPP) values: R650 16.51 GPa%, R750 12.5 GPa%, R850 9.6 GPa%, R950 4.4 GPa%.

Work hardening rate (θ) curves (Fig. 5e-f) for low-temperature rolled specimens showed a typical hump shape (rapid decrease at ε<0.02, slight rise, slow decay at ε≥0.03) due to Orowan pinning, whereas high-temperature rolled specimens showed monotonic decrease indicating lack of coordinated deformation capacity; exact numerical coordinates not provided in text.

Ostwald ripening mechanism caused carbide coarsening in R750: small carbides dissolve and diffuse via grain boundaries and dislocations to precipitate on larger carbides, producing coarse carbides at grain boundaries plus fine carbides inside grains.

In the high-temperature rolling zone, DIFT produced equiaxed ferrite grains inside honeycomb martensite lamellae; DIF grains had higher C and Mn but lower Al than original δ-ferrite, contributing to higher hardness via solid solution and Hall-Petch strengthening.
Dislocation density was calculated using the formula ρ = 2θ/(μb) where θ is average KAM, μ is step size, and b is Burgers vector.

Phase evolution with temperature: at 650–750°C carbides are stable and form C/F composite lamellae; at 850–950°C carbides fully dissolve into austenite, resulting in δ-ferrite plus honeycomb martensite lamellae after cooling.

Low-temperature rolled specimens exhibited excellent deformation coordination between hard C/F composite lamellae and soft δ-ferrite lamellae, producing serrated tensile fracture morphology with delamination characteristics (Fig. 8c).

Tensile specimens were rod-shaped with gauge length 25 mm and diameter 5 mm sampled along rolling direction; Charpy V-notch specimens were 10 mm × 10 mm × 55 mm with notch on rolled surface and impact direction perpendicular to RD; tensile crosshead speed 1.5 mm/min.
